turns.content: I'm trying to automate a file cleanup task in Rust, and I keep running into issues with object safety when defining my trait. Specifically, I defined an associated function on the trait but it won't work with dyn Trait because of the object safety rules.

That's exactly why traits cannot be used as dyn Trait if they contain associated functions or methods without a self receiver. The Rust type system enforces this to ensure that calling such methods requires knowing the concrete type at compile time, which is impossible for polymorphic dispatch through an opaque pointer. You need to remove the associated function or change generic methods into regular methods with self,

I tried removing the associated function but now I can't implement a specific method that needs to be generic over some type parameter without using dyn Trait, which seems to break object safety again.

Right, so if you need generics on every trait item method, those methods must have self as their receiver. The Rust compiler treats any method with a type parameter but no self argument as unsafe for object safety because the generic type cannot be determined at runtime when the trait is used polymorphically. You can't make a dyn Trait with such methods,
What's the best workaround then? I have a trait that needs some associated functions and some generic methods without self, but I still want to use it as a trait object.

The standard approach is to wrap your unsafe trait in a wrapper trait that only contains safe items. You define an intermediate trait with just the safe methods you need for polymorphism, then re-export or re-impl your original trait inside that wrapper so users can still access the associated functions and generic methods by dereferencing,
So I should create a WrapperTrait that has only SafeMethods, and then my UnsafeTrait with its original items lives behind it? How do I make sure the implementation is correct?

Yes, exactly. You declare impl MyUnsafeTrait for T where T: WrapperTrait, which links the concrete type to your wrapper interface. The user code only sees WrapperTrait and can use dyn Trait safely because all its items are object-safe. Your original trait remains usable internally but isn't directly exposed as a dyn trait,
One more thing though, what happens if I try to use std::any::Any with this wrapper pattern? Does it still work for the dynamic dispatch part?

Yes, once your WrapperTrait passes object safety checks by containing only safe items and methods with self, you can safely implement dyn Trait + std::any::Any. The Any trait just requires that the type is 'static and implements the trait object, which your wrapper ensures correctly without violating Rust's object safety restrictions.
